tweets.full_text:
1/ Circle's stock fell nearly 19% last week as an erstwhile little-known company Open Standard announced plans to launch Open USD, or OUSD, a stablecoin backed by nearly every finance giant you can think of, from BlackRock to Western Union.

2/ The market reacted poorly for a number of reasons. Open Standard's partnerships suggest it is targeting the same market as Circle: compliant Western enterprise payments.

But Open Standard, which will launch later this year, isn't just going after Circle's target market; Open Standard is also trying to undercut Circle while at it.

3/ Unlike Circle, Open Standard says it won't charge fees on redemption and minting. In comparison, Circle charges between 0.02% and 0.05% on redemptions depending on volume.

Open Standard also promises to give the revenue earned on reserves to partners, less a management fee.

4/ So is OUSD “an existential threat” to Circle, as Coin Bureau founder Nic Puckrin put it?

In the latest Bits + Bips, we take a look at the parts of Circle's business OUSD challenges, break down the numbers, how it is likely to play out and what to watch out for.

Full story here: https://t.co/bmlgzTaCYU

The 3% is the total you can lose in a 24h window, based on your closed balance, and it resets daily. 

So if you're down 1.5-2%, you stop, wait for the reset, and you can trade again with a fresh 3% daily DD. 

What you're describing is full-porting your whole account at 5x into one position. 

The daily drawdown exists to discourage exactly that. We'll let you do it, it's just not the optimal way to trade prop.

Most people using prop firms get this. They risk 0.5-1% per trade because they don't need to full-port max leverage. The whole point of prop is you're already working with a bigger account than you'd have on your own.

And to give you a comp outside of prop. A $1,000 account at 100x gets liqd on a 1% move.

A $100k account at Breakout costs $330-$800 depending on the eval. That fee is your entire max downside.

“One priority is to encourage households, as well as pension funds including the GPIF [Government Pension Investment Fund],  to increase their investment in Japanese financial assets."
This comment from Japan’s Finance Minister, Satsuki Katayama, contributed to a 9-basis-point decline in 10-year JGB yields and a 0.5% boost to the yen during Tokyo trading.

tweets.text:
Whenever someone says "the next billion users", we always think of them as humans.

But AI agents will be the heaviest onchain users, as humans are not capable of doing thousands of transactions per minute.

The comparison isn't even close.

Agents are basically restless, as they:

→ Quote and rebalance every few seconds
→ Pay other agents for data and compute
→ Place/pull orders across markets
→ Negotiate/settle with other agents

And much more.

Imagine a market with tens of thousands of these agents. They'll need rails that can handle that kind of volume.

Some chains can already handle heavier loads. @SuiNetwork showed it can push ~6M TPS with the new Tunnels feature.

But the space is still in its early innings for anything close to an active agentic economy.

tweets.text:
Crypto now has almost $15B of Treasury-backed assets that are not just sitting in a private database, but actually circulating onchain.

That is still tiny compared to the actual U.S. Treasury market, which has around $30.9T outstanding, which is a number I can't even fit in my head.

But anyways, it's still roughly 0.05% of the real market being represented in this form, and for crypto, this is already becoming a serious category.

On @RWA_xyz, tokenized treasuries now make up roughly half of all distributed RWA value. And the category is already led by some pretty recognizable names like:

→ @circle USYC
→ @BlackRock BUIDL
→ @OndoFinance USDY

All of them are sitting above $2B.

So IMO, the snapshot shows that one of the biggest RWA categories is becoming more usable onchain, not just more visible onchain.

- Federal Reserve policymakers will deliver price stability, according to the July Monetary Policy Report to Congress released Friday in Washington.The report noted that “measures of consumer price inflation moved up steadily” in the second half of last year in part due to tariffs, and further this year as energy prices surged in the wake of the Iran war; it also pointed out that some other measures, including the trimmed-mean measure of PCE prices that Fed Chairman Kevin Warsh has highlighted previously, declined over the past year
- The Fed says it is prepared to use its full range of tools to achieve its maximum employment and price stability goals, particularly if the federal funds rate is constrained by its effective lower bound
- The report described labor productivity growth as strong

thread_tweets.text:
My guess is humanoids will be more widespread then cars. People see the "50k-100k+" BOM cost now and think humans can do better. 

But it's about the future: I think both ubiquity/cost will end up like computers. 

And technologically, we'll see humanoids outperform humans in most physical tasks from basketball to surgery, similar to how AI was able evolve to beat humans in almost everything academic. 

So down the line, I think costs to make a humanoid at scale will keep getting compressed, optimized, and compressed down to <$5k over time. 

While underlying technology improves to displace work over a longer term horizon.

tweets.text: On this day in 1962, AT&T launched Telstar from Cape Canaveral, the world's first active communications satellite, capable of relaying 600 telephone calls or one television channel simultaneously across the Atlantic. AT&T $T funded the entire $6 million project privately and paid NASA $3 million for the launch, making it one of the first commercial ventures in space. The satellite's first live transatlantic television broadcast, between the U.S. and France, drew an audience of tens of millions and proved that real-time global communication could work as a business. Telstar operated for only seven months before radiation from high-altitude nuclear tests destroyed its transistors. The global satellite communications industry it helped create now generates over $100 billion a year in revenue.

tweets.text:
Yep, it’s interesting to witness retail capitulation.

After we got Bloomberg Meta compute article cleared up by their internal memo. 

And the 2 reports on delays were denied by Nvidia. 

Both of which caused the selloff in the first place...

So Photonics / Nvidia’s roadmap hasn’t fundamentally changed:

$LITE is still completely sold out for the next 2 years and likely into 2029.

$SIVE is about to volume ramp with  GlobalFoundries, Jabil, Poet, Ayar, and other hyperscaler suppliers.

$TSM COUPE and their TW suppliers from Shunsin to Foci aren’t randomly disappearing into the void.

$AAOI and their $471m/month h2 2027 revenue projections haven’t changed.

$IQE and their epiwafer contracts with Macom and Tower Semi haven’t disappeared.

$AXTI ownership of 40% of the InP substrate supply chains haven’t suddenly disappeared.

As with any theme from Rocketlab + Space sector 50% drop back in 2025. 

Or Nebius + Neocloud 50% drop entering 2026.

Or the Samsung/SK Hynix crash from Iran War LNG/Helium/Oil fears few months ago.

Volatility from indiscriminate thematic selloffs isn’t typically representative of individual company fundamentals.

Retail tends to build conviction over price movements and imaginary charts, then lose that when a stock drops.

And institutions are probably placing limit orders to capitalize on that.

Conviction shouldn’t be tied to the quote of a stock on a certain day, but long term revenue or operating income growth.

🇨🇳🇺🇸 #China’s Dollar Deposit Rates Top 3% on Fed Hike Bets - Securities Daily
*Chinese lenders lifted USD time deposit rates above 3% ahead of July Fed meeting, pricing in a potential US rate hike, the Securities Daily reported.Industrial Bank offers 3.2% on one-year lump-sum deposit of $8,000 or more, the report said.
*Lenders including Minsheng Banking Corp. and Bank of Jiangsu launch multi-tenor USD deposit products with entry thresholds as low as $8
*Investors should weigh rate gains against exchange volatility, suggesting diversified USD asset allocation to hedge risks, the newspaper cited Xue Hongyan, an analyst with Jiangsu Sushang Bank Co., as saying.
*Citic Securities Co. economist Ming Ming expects the Fed will more likely hold rates steady this year, and US Treasury yields could decline later amid cooling bets on a hike.
*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/Ty1uciwcrN

One thing that really stands out at $CTV.V / $CTVFF's Campbell-Crotser #fluorspar project: the grade. 👀

Historic drilling outlined 805,841 tons averaging 37.1% CaF₂.

For perspective, major global fluorspar deposits can be economic at far lower grades.

Higher raw ore grade can mean less rock moved and processed per ton of product — and potentially stronger margins.

Now they are going back with the drill bit to test it with modern drilling. ⛏️🔥

thread_tweets.text:
5/9

How Would the Fed Buy Stocks?

The Fed’s ordinary purchase authority is limited to Treasuries and government-backed mortgage securities — it cannot buy corporate bonds or equities outright.

In both 2008 and 2020, it worked around that limit the same way: the Fed set up a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV), the Treasury supplied equity as a loss-absorbing cushion, and the Fed made a secured loan to the SPV, which used the combined funds to buy the actual securities.

That financing structure — not a change in what the Fed can legally own — is what let it reach mortgage assets, corporate bonds and ETFs during past crises:

2008: The Fed used three Maiden Lane SPVs (named for the street the New York Fed sits on) to buy troubled Bear Stearns and AIG assets, plus TALF, which made loans against AAA-rated asset-backed securities.

2020: a similar alphabet soup did the same for other markets — PMCCF/SMCCF for corporate bonds and bond ETFs, plus separate facilities for money-market funds, commercial paper, PPP loans, mid-sized business loans and municipal debt.

These procedures are noted to point out the fact that it’s the Treasury, not the Fed, that would end up owning the stocks. The Fed’s role is to finance the purchase, not make it directly.

tweets.text:
Researching some stables yields now.

An interesting one are @pendle_fi PTs, and the reUSD markets.

It's 10.18% or 16.64% fixed until December, depending on which tranche you pick.

The yield comes from real-world reinsurance premiums via @re. Not emissions, not points, not someone else's leverage.

• PT-reUSD → 10.18% fixed, senior tranche, gets paid first if things go wrong

• PT-reUSDe → 16.64% fixed, mezzanine tranche, higher yield because you're absorbing losses before the senior does

So it's basically a question of what you're comfortable holding for 156 days.

I lean senior for the bulk and I'm still deciding if the extra 6% is worth the mezzanine exposure. Honestly haven't landed on it yet.

One thing worth watching are the borrow rates on Morpho.

Right now USDC borrow against PT-reUSD is sitting at 8.87%, so looping the position barely pays. But if that rate drops back to the 4$-5%, the spread reopens and you can leverage the fixed yield.

The risks are the usual PT stack. Exit before maturity and you're selling into whatever liquidity exists (reUSDe's pool is the thinner one). And you're trusting re's reinsurance book plus Pendle's contracts on top of it.

Nothing crazy here, just fixed yield with a known end date, which is kinda rare in a market where every variable rate keeps getting repriced out from under you.

tweets.text:
If Uniswap believed its liquidity was sticky, the new fee would be the same on every chain.

It isn't.

Here's what the Base discount tells you.

For context: right now LPs keep 100% of swap fees on v4. This proposal changes that across 11 chains.

Part of what LPs earn goes to Uniswap instead, gets converted, and gets burned.

The size of the cut depends on the pool. But to keep it simple:

• On most pools, Uniswap takes around $17-25 of every $100 in fees
• On the lowest fee pools it's closer to $33 of every $100
• LPs keep the rest

And remember, LPs are the ones eating impermanent loss and inventory risk. They're not exactly overpaid.

Now the interesting part.

The tax has one default rate on every chain EXCEPT Base, where it's 70% lower.

Stable pairs on Base pay a third of what every other chain pays.

Why discount one specific chain?

Because Base is Aerodrome's home turf.

It's the one place where LPs actually have somewhere better to go, and Uniswap knows it.

They're pricing like a store that only runs discounts where the competitor opened across the street. And that competitor is moving in.

Aerodrome expands to ETH mainnet this month, so the problem the discount was built for is about to follow them home.

To be fair, there's a real bull case here for UNI holders. The burn hit 186,000 UNI in a single day last month, and that was before v4 fees even existed.

Snapshot runs July 7-12. Onchain vote the week after.

The token holders vote this week. LPs vote with their capital the day it goes live.

Only one of those votes is binding.

Interesting they’re offering to take less on just one chain: the one @AerodromeFi dominates. 

A reality they’ll face on more chains #soon. 🛫

https://t.co/LiT5QL7Nf6 https://t.co/11vxK2vkOU
tweets.full_text:
If Uniswap believed its liquidity was sticky, the new fee would be the same on every chain.

It isn't.

Here's what the Base discount tells you.

For context: right now LPs keep 100% of swap fees on v4. This proposal changes that across 11 chains.

Part of what LPs earn goes to Uniswap instead, gets converted, and gets burned.

The size of the cut depends on the pool. But to keep it simple:

• On most pools, Uniswap takes around $17-25 of every $100 in fees
• On the lowest fee pools it's closer to $33 of every $100
• LPs keep the rest

And remember, LPs are the ones eating impermanent loss and inventory risk. They're not exactly overpaid.

Now the interesting part.

The tax has one default rate on every chain EXCEPT Base, where it's 70% lower.

Stable pairs on Base pay a third of what every other chain pays.

Why discount one specific chain?

Because Base is Aerodrome's home turf.

It's the one place where LPs actually have somewhere better to go, and Uniswap knows it.

They're pricing like a store that only runs discounts where the competitor opened across the street. And that competitor is moving in.

Aerodrome expands to ETH mainnet this month, so the problem the discount was built for is about to follow them home.

To be fair, there's a real bull case here for UNI holders. The burn hit 186,000 UNI in a single day last month, and that was before v4 fees even existed.

Snapshot runs July 7-12. Onchain vote the week after.

The token holders vote this week. LPs vote with their capital the day it goes live.

Only one of those votes is binding.

$CRCL extending pre-market gains +13%

Buried in today's OCC approval: Circle's new trust bank enters the custody business, targeting "banks and other financial institutions, such as regulated derivatives organizations" directly.

Interesting to consider the impact on, say, Polymarket, which settles $100B+ annualized in USDC offshore. Onshore it still settles in dollars through FCMs (futures commission merchants). OCC supervision of USDC reserves enhances the case to CFTC and counterparties that USDC is ready as an onshore settlement currency.

Big picture: USDC reserves are moving from state money transmitter oversight to OCC federal supervision. USDC is the first major stablecoin issuer to convert conditional approval to final.

Quite a few layers;

Margin Lending;

- mmBPS is slightly below standard market rates, this translates to about equivalent LTV
- Borrowcaps are below 5% of available onchain liquidity for said asset (so we can ensure available liquidity for liquidations)
- Circuit breakers allows an absolute max outflow in 2 hours less than our 1m bounty

ftUSD

- limited asset exposure, it only has exposure to USDC, USDT, stETH, and ETH
- equity based margin lending means it can fully hedge the stETH/ETH leg (this does still have stETH depeg exposure however)
- ftUSD itself is not crosschain enabled, so even if a given deployment network LST depegs it won't impact the broader market
- Circuit breakers with the same limiters

tweets.text:
Received questions around how funding on @flyingtulip_  TRS can be less than on perps even though capital efficiency is the same.

Perps target a mathematical funding rate (interest rate) of 0.01% per 8 hours, which translates to 10.95% per year. The way to think about perps are like virtual lending markets, you want to long 1 BTC, you borrow 65k USDT, buy 1 BTC, and deposit that 1 BTC as collateral. You now have a margin position. To borrow the 65k USDT you pay 10.95% interest per year. If more users want to long, borrow rates for USDT go up, interest rate go up. If more users want to short (borrow BTC, sell to USDT, deposit USDT as collateral) then interest rates go down.

So let's say you wanted to do this position on ethereum currently. The borrow cost for USDT on @flyingtulip_ is 3.41% (much less than 10.95%), so your TRS funding rate is for BTC long would be 3.41-0.01=3.40%. Inversely if you wanted to short (and since it isn't a perp, you can use BTC collateral to delta hedge), you would be getting paid +3.40%, and since you can do this at 20x leverage, that would mean ~68%.

I firmly believed the reason tradfi hasn't cared about perps is because TRS already exists in their world. And it is a better hedging system in every way. To be fair, it could not exist until now, so perps were superior, but I don't believe that statement will still be true in 5 years.

- same capital efficiency
- same leverage
- fill at all available onchain spot liquidity
- cheaper funding (a 50x btc long / short would currently be -2.99% / +3.59%)
- no ADL

Next release on @flyingtulip_

Big announcement today! =PREFERRED_MODEL("GPT‑5.6")

Management_Consulting, 43.2%,
Big_Finance_Bench, 53%
)
GPT‑5.6 SOL is becoming the preferred model across Microsoft 365 Copilot. On GDPval-AA, which evaluates the creation of real knowledge-work artifacts, it scores 1,747.8 Elo versus 1,493.7 for GPT‑5.5. It also jumps from 46.9% to 52.7% on long-running professional workflows and from 31.3% to 43.2% on management-consulting tasks.

For knowledge workers, that means turning messy context into finished work: rough notes into polished Word documents, source files into fully editable PowerPoint presentations, and raw spreadsheets into precise financial models and deeper analysis.

=FRONTIER_INTELLIGENCE * MICROSOFT_365

tweets.text:
There's about 1,000 different ironies in this Bberg article on Griffin vs. Sanders & Mamdani😂.

Why is it that when you get to the extremes of wealth and politics, it's always the most hypocritical? These guys are all just two sides of the same coin.

"Griffin questioned the growing popularity of socialism among younger Americans, arguing that history shows free-market capitalism has delivered better economic outcomes. Citing China’s shift toward open markets, he said the country’s economic reforms lifted hundreds of millions of people out of poverty.

“It’s the greatest success story in the history of humanity,” Griffin said, before adding that “whether it’s Bernie Sanders, whether it’s Mamdani,” they should “read a damn history book for once and then tell us how to run our country."
